pages.raw_text:
The meeting last night of the
Central Labor union was of more
than ordinary interest to organized
labor in eastern Washington.


Shortly after the meeting had
been called to order, Thomas Joyce
of the local bartenders' union arose
and, addressing the president, said
he was going to make a motion
which was out of the ordinary and
irregular, but hoped it would be
adopted by the delegates present"
Mr.. Joyce then moved that a COM
mittee of three be appointed by the
president to wait upon the Spokane
Trades Council, which was then in
session and invite them to attend
the meeting of the Central Labor
union and that they &c granted
seats for the evening. The motion
prevailed and Delegates Joyce,
Cooney and Schwarder were ap-
pointed. In the course of a com
paratively short time the commit
tee returned accompanied by the
members of the Trades Council
who were warmly welcomed by the
officers and members of the Central
Labor union.


The action of the central bodies
last night means that the strife
that has existed for the last two
years in the ranks of organized la
Dor in Spokane is ended and that
hereafter the unions of Spokane
will present a united front all along
the line. The meeting of last night
has created great enthusiasm
among trade unionists in this com
munity. Before the meeting closed
an invitation was extended TO the
visitors to meet in the future with
the Central labor union.
